title: Threads, tasks and executors

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Threads, tasks and executors; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; JVM/runtime concepts, Concurrency, and Virtual Threads learning paths; atomic topic: Threads, tasks and executors
sources.claim: Java tasks describe units of work, while executors own scheduling, worker resources, result futures, cancellation, and an explicit shutdown lifecycle.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java tasks describe units of work, while executors own scheduling, worker resources, result futures, cancellation, and an explicit shutdown lifecycle.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A fixed executor runs three independent Callable tasks, collects each Future result, handles failure, and shuts down in a finally path. Your goal is to run independent tasks and collect results while shutting down resources correctly. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Packages, builds and tests; List, Set, Map and Queue; Exception flow and stack traces; Functional interfaces and lambdas. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java tasks describe units of work, while executors own scheduling, worker resources, result futures, cancellation, and an explicit shutdown lifecycle. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Submitting does not guarantee immediate start or success, Future.get can block or wrap failure, cancellation is cooperative, unbounded queues can exhaust memory, and executor threads keep a process alive. The learner must reject this documented misconception: ExecutorService automatically shuts down when submitted tasks finish, and Future.get always returns a value promptly.

includes: Express units of work and manage execution through structured executor boundaries.
Run independent tasks and collect results while shutting down resources correctly.
excludes: Submitting does not guarantee immediate start or success, Future.get can block or wrap failure, cancellation is cooperative, unbounded queues can exhaust memory, and executor threads keep a process alive.

outcomes: Run independent tasks and collect results while shutting down resources correctly.

examples.title: Threads, tasks and executors: direct language case
examples.situation: A fixed executor runs three independent Callable tasks, collects each Future result, handles failure, and shuts down in a finally path.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A fixed executor runs three independent Callable tasks, collects each Future result, handles failure, and shuts down in a finally path.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Own the executor lifecycle explicitly: bound work and waiting, inspect task failure, request cancellation when needed, and shut down or close the executor.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Own the executor lifecycle explicitly: bound work and waiting, inspect task failure, request cancellation when needed, and shut down or close the executor.

examples.title: Threads, tasks and executors: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A timeout cancels remaining work and awaits termination instead of abandoning live executor threads after the first failure.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A timeout cancels remaining work and awaits termination instead of abandoning live executor threads after the first failure.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Submitting does not guarantee immediate start or success, Future.get can block or wrap failure, cancellation is cooperative, unbounded queues can exhaust memory, and executor threads keep a process alive.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: ExecutorService automatically shuts down when submitted tasks finish, and Future.get always returns a value promptly.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Run independent tasks and collect results while shutting down resources correctly.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: ExecutorService automatically shuts down when submitted tasks finish, and Future.get always returns a value promptly.
misconceptions.correction: Own the executor lifecycle explicitly: bound work and waiting, inspect task failure, request cancellation when needed, and shut down or close the executor.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: ExecutorService automatically shuts down when submitted tasks finish, and Future.get always returns a value promptly.

analogy.example: a dispatch center that tracks reachable work orders and assigns bounded tasks while protecting shared ledgers can introduce Threads, tasks and executors, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reachability, executors, races, and virtual-thread workload choice
limitations: memory visibility, scheduling, atomicity, cancellation, and garbage-collection timing cannot be inferred from human dispatch

recap.summary: You should now be able to run independent tasks and collect results while shutting down resources correctly. Keep this boundary in view: Submitting does not guarantee immediate start or success, Future.get can block or wrap failure, cancellation is cooperative, unbounded queues can exhaust memory, and executor threads keep a process alive.
